questions.text: I hold the view that suffering which is visible to me and within my capacity to address constitutes a moral obligation, not merely an opportunity.
I feel most at ease when expectations are set collaboratively.
I prioritize long-term wellbeing over short-term pleasure when the two conflict.
I believe that overthinking kills momentum and that action teaches faster than analysis.
I find that I am more attuned to ideas being discussed than to the feelings in the room.
I routinely evaluate relationships by whether they support my personal growth.
I notice when someone positions a request as a suggestion to make it more palatable.
I find that emotional distance from a crisis situation allows me to identify the highest-impact actions more accurately.
I regularly step back so others have space to take initiative.
I find that my internal world is quiet and spacious rather than intense and crowded.
I feel most like myself when I am in the middle of helping someone.
I prefer working in environments where roles and expectations are explicit rather than inferred.
I believe that pursuing personal excellence is a worthy way to spend my life.
I experience gratitude as a quiet contentment rather than an overwhelming surge.
I view taking charge as a service to the group.
I believe that remaining emotionally uninvolved during others' crises is a legitimate and often necessary approach to maximizing positive impact.
I prefer handling each situation freshly rather than automating it into a routine.
When I get good news, the euphoria can last for days.
